text: The surgeon identifies and begins to take down adhesions around the gallbladder using robotic instruments, carefully separating the fibrous tissue from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a clip applier to retract the liver, demonstrating the initial step in the procedure.
The surgeon uses the clip applier to take down the medial attachments of the gallbladder, resting the tip into the gallbladder bed to minimize the risk of liver laceration.
The surgeon removes remaining adhesions and begins dissection in the hepatocytic triangle, carefully separating tissue structures in this area.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum over the paddle cystic triangle, initiating exposure of the cystic duct and cystic artery.
The cystic duct becomes visible as the surgeon continues dissection in the hepatocytic triangle, revealing its anatomical position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon separates the medial attachments of the gallbladder, carefully dissecting the connective tissue to further expose the gallbladder from surrounding structures.
The surgeon separates the medial attachments, allowing the gallbladder to be pulled further away, which opens up the hepatocystic triangle for better visualization.
The surgeon uses a clipper bar to provide counter tension, aiding in the visualization and manipulation of the cystic duct.
The surgeon uses their left hand to apply tension on the tissue, aiding in the visualization and manipulation of the gallbladder during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery and cystic duct, aiming to achieve the critical view of safety by ensuring clear visualization of these structures within the hepatocystic triangle.
The surgeon completes the critical view of safety as the peritoneum of the gallbladder visibly spreads, indicating clear exposure of the relevant anatomical structures.
The surgeon removes a significant portion of the gallbladder, utilizing robotic instruments to facilitate the process with ease.
The surgeon leaves a small portion of the gallbladder attached to facilitate clipping of the cystic duct and artery, while simultaneously removing the remaining gallbladder tissue from the liver.
The surgeon positions the clip-up bar away from the falciform ligament to prevent it from bumping the structure during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a hook instrument to divide the cystic artery while the clip-up bar is being reloaded.
The surgeon removes part of the remaining gallbladder from the liver bed while waiting for the clip to reload, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the tissue.
The surgeon divides the remaining attachments of the gallbladder from the liver, ensuring safety and ease by leaving only the top attachments without requiring counter traction.
The surgeon activates the Firefly imaging system to check for any bile leaks from the gallbladder bed, ensuring the surgical field is clear of leaks.
The surgeon applies a third clip to the cystic duct and then divides it using the cut setting of the monopolar instrument.
The surgeon removes the hook instrument and inserts the endocache device through the same port, preparing to secure the gallbladder.
The surgeon inserts the hook through the umbilical port, positioning it for further manipulation within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon extracts the gallbladder through the belly button incision using robotic assistance, following the division of the cystic duct.
